cards.text: The four stream state flags are: {{c1::goodbit}} (everything ok), {{c2::badbit}} (fatal error), {{c3::failbit}} (non-fatal error), and {{c4::eofbit}} (end of file).

cards.front: What causes the failbit to be set?
cards.back: Non-fatal errors like type mismatches (e.g., trying to read a letter into an int variable) or trying to open a non-existent file.

cards.front: What causes the badbit to be set?
cards.back: Fatal errors like reading from a closed stream, running out of memory, or unrecoverable hardware failures.

cards.text: To check if a stream is in a good state, use {{c1::if (std::cin.good())}} or {{c2::if (std::cin)}}.
To check for specific error flags: {{c1::cin.fail()}} for failbit, {{c2::cin.bad()}} for badbit, {{c3::cin.eof()}} for eofbit.
To reset all stream error flags, use {{c1::std::cin.clear()}}.

cards.question: What happens with this code if the user enters "abc"?
cards.answer: x remains uninitialized (garbage value), std::cin enters fail state, and the invalid input "abc" stays in the buffer.
cards.explanation: When extraction fails, the variable is left unchanged and the stream's failbit is set.

When validating input:
  • Check if extraction succeeded (if (cin >> x))
  • If failed: clear() to reset flags
  • Use ignore() to remove bad input from buffer
  • Prompt user to try again

cards.front: How can you use a stream in a boolean context?
cards.back: Streams can be used in boolean contexts (if statements, while loops). They evaluate to true if in good state, false if any error flag is set.

cards.question: What does this idiom do?
cards.answer: Reads values until extraction fails or EOF is reached. The stream evaluates to false when an error occurs or input ends.
cards.explanation: Common pattern for processing input until it runs out or encounters an error.

cards.front: Why doesn't clear() remove bad input from the stream buffer?
cards.back: clear() only resets error flags. Bad input remains in the buffer. You must use ignore() to actually remove the invalid characters.

cards.question: What's wrong with this error handling?
cards.answer: Missing cin.ignore() - the invalid input stays in buffer, causing an infinite loop.
cards.explanation: Without ignore(), the same invalid input is read repeatedly, continuously failing.
